title: Aspect fundamentals
subtitle: What perfective and imperfective really mean

vocabulary.meaning: to do (impf.) / to do, finish doing (pf.)
vocabulary.exampleCyrillic: Я делаю это каждый день. / Я сделал это.
vocabulary.exampleTransliteration: Ya delayu eto kazhdiy den'. / Ya sdelal eto.
vocabulary.exampleMeaning: I do this every day. / I did this (and finished).

vocabulary.meaning: to read (impf.) / to read through, finish reading (pf.)
vocabulary.exampleCyrillic: Я читаю книгу. / Я прочитал книгу.
vocabulary.exampleTransliteration: Ya chitayu knigu. / Ya prochital knigu.
vocabulary.exampleMeaning: I am reading a book. / I read the book (finished it).

vocabulary.meaning: to write (impf.) / to write, finish writing (pf.)
vocabulary.exampleCyrillic: Я пишу письмо. / Я написал письмо.
vocabulary.exampleTransliteration: Ya pishu pis'mo. / Ya napisal pis'mo.
vocabulary.exampleMeaning: I am writing a letter. / I wrote the letter (completed).

vocabulary.meaning: to speak / to say (a single thing, completed)
vocabulary.exampleCyrillic: Он говорит по-русски. / Он сказал правду.
vocabulary.exampleTransliteration: On govorit po-russki. / On skazal pravdu.
vocabulary.exampleMeaning: He speaks Russian. / He said the truth.

hook.text: You already know that Russian verbs come in pairs — imperfective and perfective. You've seen делать/сделать, читать/прочитать. Now you'll understand exactly what the difference means and when to use each. This is one of the most conceptually important topics in Russian — once it clicks, your whole understanding of the verb system shifts.

why.text: English has one verb form ('read') that covers both 'I was reading' and 'I read it through'. Russian splits this into two separate verbs. The imperfective describes the activity itself — the process, the habit, the ongoing state. The perfective describes the result — the completion, the single event, the moment it was finished. This isn't grammar complexity for its own sake — it's the language encoding something real about how events work.

cards.title: The core distinction
cards.body: Imperfective (НСВ): describes the action as a process, habit, or repeated event — 'was doing', 'does regularly', 'kept doing'. Perfective (СВ): describes the action as a completed whole — 'did and finished', 'managed to do', 'a single completed event'. The question is not WHEN but HOW you see the action.

examples.meaning: I was reading a book. (process — may not be finished)
I read the book. (done — finished it)
He was writing a letter. (activity in progress)
He wrote the letter. (completed, sent, done)

cards.title: Present tense = imperfective only
cards.body: This is a hard rule: only imperfective verbs have a present tense. Perfective verbs have no present — their 'present-looking' form is actually future. If you see a verb in present tense, it is imperfective. No exception.

examples.meaning: I am reading / I read (imperfective present)
I will read / I will finish reading (perfective future)
I am doing / I do (imperfective present)
I will do / I will finish doing (perfective future)

cards.title: Key aspect pairs
cards.body: Aspect pairs are usually formed by: (1) adding a prefix to the imperfective (читать → прочитать), or (2) a completely different root (говорить → сказать). Some pairs share a root but differ by suffix. Learn them together as a unit, not as separate verbs.

questions.prompt: Which aspect has no present tense — imperfective or perfective?
questions.explanation: Perfective verbs have no present tense. A perfective verb in 'present' form is actually future tense.

questions.prompt: I read the book (and finished it): Я ___ книгу.
questions.explanation: прочитал (perfective past) = read and finished. читал = was reading (process, may not be done).
уже (uzhe) = already. Common with perfective verbs: Я уже сделал = I already did it.

cards.title: Context clues for aspect choice
cards.body: Certain adverbs push you toward one aspect. Imperfective triggers: каждый день (every day), всегда (always), часто (often), долго (for a long time), обычно (usually). Perfective triggers: уже (already), наконец (finally), вдруг (suddenly), быстро (quickly, once).

examples.meaning: I read the newspaper every day. (habit → imperfective)
I already read the newspaper. (completed → perfective)

cards.title: говорить vs сказать — a suppletive pair
cards.body: говорить/сказать is an unusual pair — completely different roots (suppletive). говорить = to speak (general ability, ongoing). сказать = to say (one specific thing, one moment). This mirrors English 'speak' vs 'say'.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means 'I was writing a letter (process)'?
questions.explanation: писал (imperfective past) = was writing, process. написал (perfective past) = wrote and finished.

questions.prompt: What is the perfective of читать?

questions.explanation: каждый день = every day. Triggers imperfective aspect.

questions.prompt: I already finished doing it: Я уже ___ это.
Does the perfective have a present tense?
options: No — the perfective has no present tense
questions.answer: No — the perfective has no present tense
questions.explanation: Perfective verbs have no present. Their 'present-looking' conjugation is future tense.
говорить = general ability to speak (imperfective). сказать = say one specific thing (perfective).

rules: Imperfective (НСВ): process, habit, repeated event — 'was doing', 'does regularly'
Perfective (СВ): completed whole — 'did and finished', 'managed to do once'
Present tense = imperfective only. No exceptions.
Perfective 'present-looking' form is actually future tense
Context clues: каждый день/всегда → imperfective; уже/наконец → perfective
summary.nextLesson: Next: aspect in the past tense — the full past system with both aspects.

questions.prompt: Which verb means 'I was reading' (process, not finished)?
Does the perfective aspect have a present tense?
options: No — perfective has no present tense
questions.answer: No — perfective has no present tense

questions.prompt: What is the perfective of писать?
I already finished it: Я уже ___.
Every day I read: Каждый день я ___.
